title: Emergency department screening tool predicts firearm violence risk
study_design: Prospective longitudinal study at 4 level 1 emergency departments in 3 cities
primary_outcome: Firearm violence (shooting someone or being shot) at 12 months
source_summary_bullets: The SaFETy score effectively predicted firearm violence risk among young adults in the emergency department
A cutoff above 0 optimized sensitivity at 83.1% while maintaining 62.4% specificity
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: This screening tool could help ED physicians identify high-risk patients for targeted violence prevention interventions and appropriate referrals
limitations: Outcomes were primarily self-reported
Highest-risk subsamples were more likely to have missing data
25.5% loss to follow-up
bottom_line: The SaFETy score effectively stratifies firearm violence risk in young ED patients and could guide prevention efforts.
why_this_matters: Emergency departments see high-risk populations for firearm violence, and effective screening tools could enable targeted prevention interventions.
population: Adults aged 18 to 24 years seen in an ED for any reason (1506 participants, 61.4% female, mean age 21.3 years)
comparator: Baseline covariates and comprehensive covariate set
key_results: Among 1122 participants with follow-up data, 73 (6.5%) had 12-month firearm violence
12-month firearm violence rates by SaFETy score: 0 points (1.8%), 1-5 points (12.1%), 6+ points (25.0%)
Area under ROC curve was 0.78 (95% CI 0.72-0.83)
Optimal cut point >0 had sensitivity 83.1% and specificity 62.4%
Adding SaFETy score to full covariate set improved AUC from 0.81 to 0.84 (p=0.025)
clinical_interpretation: The SaFETy score provides a practical screening tool for identifying young adults at high risk for firearm violence
The strong risk gradient suggests potential utility for targeted interventions, though implementation would require robust prevention programs

title: FDA Issues Class I Recall for Contaminated Metformin Batches
summary: The FDA has issued a Class I recall for several batches of metformin extended-release tablets due to NDMA contamination levels exceeding acceptable limits. The recall affects multiple generic manufacturers and represents the most serious type of recall indicating reasonable probability of serious adverse health consequences.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists frequently encounter patients on metformin and need to identify affected batches, consider alternative diabetes management, and counsel patients on medication safety.

title: New Evidence Questions Lactate Clearance Targets in Sepsis Management
summary: A large multicenter study challenges current lactate clearance targets in sepsis protocols, finding no significant mortality benefit from achieving 20% lactate clearance within 6 hours compared to standard care. The findings question whether aggressive lactate-directed therapy improves outcomes in septic patients.
why_it_matters: This evidence may influence sepsis protocols and quality metrics that hospitalists use daily, potentially reducing unnecessary interventions while maintaining optimal patient outcomes.

title: AI Tool for Pneumonia Severity Assessment Shows Promise in ED Validation Study
summary: An artificial intelligence tool demonstrated superior accuracy compared to traditional severity scores in predicting 30-day mortality and need for intensive care in emergency department patients with pneumonia. The tool analyzes chest X-rays, vital signs, and laboratory values to provide real-time risk stratification.
why_it_matters: This technology could help hospitalists make more accurate triage decisions and optimize resource allocation for pneumonia patients, potentially improving outcomes while reducing unnecessary ICU admissions.

title: Critical Shortage of Fidaxomicin Prompts Updated C. diff Treatment Recommendations
summary: A nationwide shortage of fidaxomicin has prompted infectious disease societies to issue updated treatment algorithms for Clostridioides difficile infections. The shortage is expected to last through Q2 2026, requiring clinicians to rely more heavily on vancomycin and consider fecal microbiota transplantation earlier in recurrent cases.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists managing C. diff infections need to adjust treatment protocols immediately, with increased emphasis on vancomycin optimization and earlier consideration of alternative therapies for recurrent infections.

title: Early surgical valve replacement offers survival benefit in asymptomatic, very severe aortic stenosis

bottom_line: Early surgical valve replacement significantly reduces 10-year cardiovascular mortality in asymptomatic very severe aortic stenosis compared to conservative management.

title: FDA Issues Safety Alert for Generic Drug Manufacturing Issues
summary: The FDA has issued a safety alert regarding manufacturing defects identified at several generic drug facilities, affecting commonly prescribed medications including blood pressure and diabetes drugs. The alert includes recommendations for healthcare providers to monitor patients for potential therapeutic failures and adverse events.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists need to be aware of potential therapeutic failures in patients taking affected generic medications and may need to consider switching to alternative formulations or brand-name drugs.

title: New Sepsis Biomarker Shows Promise for Early Detection in ED
summary: A large multicenter validation study demonstrates that a novel biomarker panel can identify sepsis up to 6 hours earlier than current methods, with 89% sensitivity and 94% specificity. The biomarker test is now being implemented in emergency departments across major health systems for real-world validation.
why_it_matters: Earlier sepsis detection could significantly improve patient outcomes and reduce mortality, making this a potentially practice-changing development for emergency medicine and critical care physicians.

title: Updated Guidelines for Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia Prevention Released
summary: The Infectious Diseases Society of America has released updated guidelines for preventing hospital-acquired pneumonia, emphasizing new evidence-based strategies for ventilator-associated pneumonia prevention and updated antibiotic prophylaxis recommendations. The guidelines include specific protocols for immunocompromised patients and those with prolonged hospital stays.
why_it_matters: These updated guidelines provide hospitalists with evidence-based strategies to reduce one of the most common and serious hospital-acquired infections, potentially improving patient outcomes and reducing healthcare costs.

title: AI-Guided Insulin Dosing Reduces Hypoglycemia in Hospitalized Patients
summary: A randomized controlled trial of 1,200 hospitalized patients found that an AI-guided insulin dosing algorithm reduced severe hypoglycemic episodes by 47% compared to standard sliding scale protocols while maintaining similar glycemic control. The system integrates real-time glucose monitoring with patient-specific factors to optimize insulin dosing.
why_it_matters: This technology could transform inpatient diabetes management by reducing dangerous hypoglycemic events while maintaining effective glucose control, offering hospitalists a safer approach to insulin management.

title: Updated Vancomycin Dosing Guidelines Released by IDSA
summary: The Infectious Diseases Society of America has released updated guidelines for vancomycin dosing, emphasizing AUC-guided dosing over trough-based monitoring. The new recommendations include specific protocols for critically ill patients and those with renal impairment.
why_it_matters: These guidelines will require hospitalists to modify their approach to vancomycin monitoring and may necessitate collaboration with pharmacy services for AUC calculations.

title: New COVID-19 Variant BA.6 Drives Spring Hospitalization Surge

why_it_matters: Hospitalists should prepare for increased COVID-19 admissions and consider updated isolation protocols, while remaining vigilant for breakthrough infections in vaccinated patients.

why_it_matters: Hospitalists ordering contrast studies will need to reassess risk-benefit ratios and implement stricter pre-procedural kidney function screening protocols.

title: FDA Approves New Antibiotic for Resistant Infections

why_it_matters: This provides hospitalists with a new treatment option for critically ill patients with resistant infections, potentially improving outcomes in sepsis and healthcare-associated infections.

title: Updated Sepsis Guidelines Release New Recommendations
summary: New international sepsis guidelines provide updated recommendations for early recognition, antibiotic timing, and hemodynamic management. The guidelines emphasize personalized approaches to fluid resuscitation and introduce new biomarker-guided therapy recommendations.
why_it_matters: These guidelines directly impact daily practice for hospitalists and intensivists, potentially changing standard approaches to sepsis recognition and management protocols.

title: New COVID Variant Drives Hospitalization Surge
summary: A newly identified COVID-19 variant has led to increased hospitalizations across multiple regions, with higher rates of severe disease in immunocompromised patients. Health officials are monitoring vaccine effectiveness against the variant and updating treatment protocols.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists need to be aware of changing COVID epidemiology and may need to adjust isolation protocols and treatment approaches for admitted patients.

title: AKI Prevention Guidelines Updated with New Evidence
summary: Updated acute kidney injury prevention guidelines incorporate new evidence on biomarker-guided interventions and medication adjustments. The guidelines provide specific recommendations for high-risk hospitalized patients and perioperative care.
why_it_matters: AKI is a common complication in hospitalized patients, and these updated guidelines provide evidence-based strategies that hospitalists can implement to reduce kidney injury risk.

title: Updated Heart Failure Guidelines Emphasize Early SGLT2 Inhibitor Use
summary: New American Heart Association guidelines recommend initiating SGLT2 inhibitors within 30 days of heart failure diagnosis, regardless of diabetes status. The guidelines also expand indications for combination therapy with ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists in hospitalized patients.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists managing acute heart failure will need to consider earlier initiation of SGLT2 inhibitors during admission, potentially improving outcomes and reducing readmissions.

title: FDA Issues Class I Recall for Contaminated Amlodipine Tablets
summary: The FDA announced a voluntary Class I recall of amlodipine besylate tablets due to potential contamination with carcinogenic impurities. The recall affects multiple generic manufacturers and lot numbers distributed nationwide over the past six months.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists should verify current amlodipine prescriptions and may need to switch patients to alternative calcium channel blockers or adjust antihypertensive regimens during admission.

title: CDC Reports Surge in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacteriaceae Infections
summary: New CDC surveillance data shows a 40% increase in carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae infections in hospitalized patients over the past year. The highest rates are reported in intensive care units and among patients with prolonged hospital stays or recent antibiotic exposure.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists should heighten surveillance for CRE infections and consider empiric coverage in high-risk patients, while reinforcing antibiotic stewardship practices to prevent further resistance development.

title: Large-Scale Validation Study Confirms AI Tool Reduces Sepsis Mortality
summary: A multicenter validation study of 50,000 patients demonstrated that an AI-powered sepsis detection system reduced in-hospital mortality by 18% and decreased time to antibiotic administration by 1.2 hours. The system showed consistent performance across different hospital systems and patient populations.
why_it_matters: This validation data supports broader implementation of AI sepsis detection tools in hospital systems, potentially improving early recognition and treatment of sepsis in hospitalized patients.

title: New Evidence for Device Therapy in Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction
summary: Recent clinical trial data demonstrates significant mortality benefits from cardiac resynchronization therapy in select patients with heart failure and preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). The study showed a 23% reduction in cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalizations over 18 months in patients with specific ECG criteria and functional limitations despite optimal medical therapy.
why_it_matters: This represents a potential paradigm shift in HFpEF management, as device therapy has traditionally been reserved for reduced ejection fraction patients. Hospitalists will need to identify appropriate candidates for cardiology referral.

title: Updated Antibiotic Stewardship Guidelines Address Rising Resistance Patterns
summary: The Infectious Diseases Society of America released updated antibiotic stewardship guidelines incorporating new resistance data and diagnostic tools. Key changes include revised empiric therapy recommendations for hospital-acquired pneumonia and updated duration guidelines for common infections based on biomarker-guided protocols.
why_it_matters: These guidelines directly impact daily antibiotic prescribing decisions for hospitalists and provide evidence-based frameworks for optimizing therapy duration and selection in the era of increasing antimicrobial resistance.

title: Novel Biomarkers Show Promise for Early AKI Detection in Hospitalized Patients
summary: A multicenter validation study of urinary biomarkers demonstrates superior performance compared to serum creatinine for detecting acute kidney injury 12-24 hours earlier in hospitalized patients. The biomarker panel showed 89% sensitivity and 84% specificity for predicting AKI requiring renal replacement therapy within 72 hours.
why_it_matters: Earlier AKI detection could enable prompt intervention to prevent progression to severe kidney injury. This may soon change how hospitalists monitor at-risk patients and guide nephrology consultation timing.

title: FDA Issues Safety Communication on SGLT2 Inhibitors and Severe Hypoglycemia Risk
summary: The FDA issued a safety communication warning about increased risk of severe hypoglycemia when SGLT2 inhibitors are used with insulin or sulfonylureas in hospitalized patients with acute illness. The alert follows reports of 47 serious adverse events, including three deaths, in patients with concurrent infections or reduced oral intake.
why_it_matters: This safety alert requires immediate attention to medication reconciliation practices for hospitalized diabetic patients. Hospitalists must carefully evaluate SGLT2 inhibitor continuation during acute illness and consider dose adjustments or temporary discontinuation.

why_it_matters: This helps hospitalists counsel patients about expected outcomes with Paxlovid treatment and manage expectations about potential symptom recurrence without indicating treatment failure.

title: FDA Approves New Alzheimer's Drug
summary: The FDA has approved a new treatment for Alzheimer's disease, marking a significant advancement in dementia care. The approval comes after clinical trials demonstrated efficacy in slowing cognitive decline in patients with mild to moderate disease.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists frequently encounter patients with dementia and cognitive impairment. This new treatment option may impact discharge planning and long-term care decisions for hospitalized patients with Alzheimer's disease.

title: CDC Issues Alert on Antibiotic Resistance
summary: The CDC has issued a nationwide alert regarding emerging patterns of antibiotic resistance in common hospital-acquired infections. The alert highlights specific resistance mechanisms and recommends enhanced surveillance and antimicrobial stewardship measures.
why_it_matters: This directly impacts daily antibiotic prescribing decisions for hospitalists and may require adjustments to empiric therapy protocols for suspected infections in hospitalized patients.

title: FDA Warning on Cardiac Device Safety
summary: The FDA has issued a safety warning regarding potential malfunctions in certain cardiac devices, including specific pacemaker and defibrillator models. Healthcare providers are advised to monitor patients closely and follow updated protocols for device interrogation.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists frequently care for patients with cardiac devices and need to be aware of potential device-related complications that could affect patient safety and require immediate intervention.

title: FDA Warns of Dangerous Counterfeit Ozempic Pens in US Supply Chain
summary: The FDA has issued an urgent warning about counterfeit Ozempic pens containing unknown substances that have entered the US supply chain. Several patients have been hospitalized after using fake semaglutide products purchased from unauthorized sources.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists may encounter patients with unexplained adverse reactions from counterfeit diabetes medications, requiring careful medication history taking and awareness of this safety issue.

title: Updated AHA/ASA Guidelines Extend Thrombectomy Window to 24 Hours for Selected Patients
summary: New American Heart Association and American Stroke Association guidelines have extended the therapeutic window for mechanical thrombectomy to 24 hours for carefully selected stroke patients with favorable imaging characteristics. The updated recommendations are based on recent clinical trial data showing benefit in extended time windows.
why_it_matters: This guideline change significantly impacts acute stroke care in hospitals, potentially allowing more patients to receive life-saving thrombectomy procedures and requiring updated protocols for stroke evaluation.

title: Large RCT Shows 5-Day Antibiotic Course Non-Inferior to 10 Days for Community-Acquired Pneumonia
summary: A multicenter randomized controlled trial involving over 3,000 patients demonstrated that a 5-day course of antibiotics was non-inferior to the standard 10-day treatment for hospitalized patients with community-acquired pneumonia. The shorter course showed similar clinical cure rates with reduced antibiotic exposure and healthcare costs.
why_it_matters: This evidence supports shorter antibiotic durations for CAP, potentially reducing antibiotic resistance, adverse effects, and healthcare costs while maintaining clinical efficacy in hospitalized patients.

title: FDA Announces Critical Shortage of Key Diabetes Medications Due to Manufacturing Issues
summary: The FDA has reported a critical shortage of several widely-used diabetes medications including metformin and certain insulin formulations due to manufacturing plant shutdowns. The shortage is expected to last 3-6 months, prompting recommendations for alternative treatment regimens and careful medication reconciliation for hospitalized patients.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists will need to modify diabetes management protocols and ensure proper medication reconciliation for discharged patients, potentially requiring consultation with endocrinology for complex cases and alternative treatment plans.

title: Finerenone Shows Promise for Type 1 Diabetes with CKD
summary: New clinical trial data suggests finerenone, a selective mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, may provide renal and cardiovascular benefits in patients with type 1 diabetes and chronic kidney disease. The study demonstrated significant reduction in albuminuria and preservation of kidney function compared to placebo, with a favorable safety profile including lower rates of hyperkalemia than traditional ACE inhibitors or ARBs.
why_it_matters: This represents a potential new therapeutic option for hospitalists managing diabetic nephropathy in type 1 diabetes patients, a population with limited evidence-based treatment options beyond ACE inhibitors and ARBs.

title: Head-to-Head Comparison: Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban Bleeding Risk
summary: A large real-world comparative effectiveness study found apixaban associated with significantly lower rates of major bleeding compared to rivaroxaban in patients treated for venous thromboembolism. The analysis included over 50,000 patients and showed a 23% reduction in major bleeding events with apixaban while maintaining similar efficacy for VTE prevention.
why_it_matters: This data provides important guidance for hospitalists selecting DOACs for VTE treatment, suggesting apixaban may be the safer choice for patients at higher bleeding risk.

title: Intensive BP Control in ICH Improves Functional Recovery
summary: A multicenter randomized trial demonstrated that intensive blood pressure control (target <140 mmHg) within 6 hours of intracerebral hemorrhage onset significantly improved functional outcomes at 90 days compared to standard care. The intervention group showed better modified Rankin Scale scores and reduced hematoma expansion without increasing adverse events.
why_it_matters: This provides clear evidence for aggressive BP management in acute ICH, changing the approach for emergency physicians and intensivists managing these critically ill patients in the first hours after presentation.

title: Delayed Pegfilgrastim Timing Reduces Severe Bone Pain
summary: A randomized study found that delaying pegfilgrastim administration to 3-5 days post-chemotherapy significantly reduced severe bone pain compared to standard next-day dosing, without compromising neutropenia prevention efficacy. Patients receiving delayed dosing reported 40% less severe bone pain and required fewer analgesics.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists managing oncology patients can improve quality of life and reduce analgesic requirements by adjusting pegfilgrastim timing, particularly relevant for inpatient chemotherapy regimens.

why_it_matters: Hospitalists and internists should maintain heightened cardiovascular surveillance in COVID-19 survivors and consider more aggressive cardiovascular risk factor modification in this population.

title: AI-Powered Sepsis Detection Tool Shows Promise in Large Hospital Study
summary: A multi-center validation study of an artificial intelligence-based sepsis early warning system demonstrated improved detection rates and reduced time to treatment initiation. why_it_matters: Early sepsis detection remains critical for hospitalists and emergency physicians, and AI-assisted tools may soon become standard practice for improving patient outcomes and reducing sepsis mortality.

title: Tecovirimat Shows Limited Efficacy Against Mpox in Clinical Trial
summary: A randomized controlled trial found that tecovirimat did not significantly reduce lesion healing time or viral shedding duration in patients with mpox compared to placebo. The antiviral drug, which was widely used during the 2022 mpox outbreak based on animal studies and compassionate use data, showed minimal clinical benefit in this definitive human trial.
why_it_matters: This challenges current treatment approaches for mpox patients and may lead to revised treatment guidelines, particularly for hospitalized patients with severe disease.

title: Sivelestat Reduces ARDS Risk After Cardiac Surgery
summary: A multicenter trial demonstrated that sivelestat, a neutrophil elastase inhibitor, significantly reduced the incidence of acute respiratory distress syndrome in patients undergoing high-risk cardiac surgery. The drug showed particular benefit in patients with prolonged cardiopulmonary bypass times and complex procedures.
why_it_matters: This represents a potential breakthrough in ARDS prevention for cardiac surgery patients, offering intensivists and cardiac teams a new tool to reduce one of the most serious postoperative complications.

title: Local Anesthesia for Burr Hole Surgery Reduces Postoperative Delirium
summary: A comparative study found that performing burr hole procedures under local anesthesia instead of general anesthesia significantly reduced the incidence of postoperative delirium in elderly patients with subdural hematomas. Patients who received local anesthesia had shorter hospital stays and better cognitive outcomes.
why_it_matters: This finding could change surgical approach decisions for elderly patients requiring burr hole procedures, potentially reducing delirium rates and improving outcomes in a vulnerable population commonly managed by hospitalists.

title: New Guidelines Expand Neoadjuvant Therapy for Colon Cancer
summary: Updated clinical practice guidelines now recommend neoadjuvant chemotherapy for a broader range of colon cancer patients, including those with T4 tumors and select T3 cases with high-risk features. The guidelines cite improved surgical outcomes and reduced recurrence rates as key benefits of this approach.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists managing cancer patients need to understand these updated treatment sequences, as they may encounter more patients receiving chemotherapy before surgery and need to coordinate perioperative care accordingly.

title: RSV Vaccine in Pregnancy Shows Strong Effectiveness Against Infant Hospitalization
summary: A large real-world study demonstrates that maternal RSV vaccination during pregnancy significantly reduces RSV-related hospitalizations in infants during their first 6 months of life. The vaccine showed approximately 70% effectiveness against severe RSV disease requiring hospitalization, with the greatest protection observed in the first 3 months after birth.
why_it_matters: This data supports routine RSV vaccination counseling for pregnant patients and provides evidence for hospitalists managing RSV cases in infants to inquire about maternal vaccination status.

title: Carbapenem-Resistant Infections Rise 15% in US Hospitals
summary: CDC surveillance data reveals a concerning 15% increase in carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections across US hospitals over the past year. The rise is most pronounced in ICU settings and among immunocompromised patients, with mortality rates approaching 40% in severe cases.
why_it_matters: Hospitalists must maintain high suspicion for resistant organisms when selecting empiric antibiotics, particularly in ICU patients, and ensure rapid implementation of contact precautions and antimicrobial stewardship protocols.

title: AI Sepsis Detection Tool Reduces Hospital Mortality by 18% in Validation Study
summary: A multi-center validation study of an AI-powered early warning system for sepsis detection showed an 18% reduction in hospital mortality when implemented across 12 hospitals. The system analyzes vital signs, laboratory values, and clinical notes in real-time to identify patients at risk for sepsis up to 6 hours before traditional recognition methods.
why_it_matters: This represents a significant advance in sepsis care that could be implemented in hospital systems, requiring hospitalists to understand how to integrate AI alerts into clinical decision-making workflows.

title: New Guidelines Recommend Higher BP Targets for Frail Elderly Patients
summary: Updated consensus guidelines from major cardiology and geriatrics societies now recommend blood pressure targets of <150/90 mmHg for frail elderly patients over 80 years old, rather than the standard <130/80 mmHg target. The change reflects new evidence showing that aggressive BP control in frail patients may increase falls and cognitive decline without cardiovascular benefit.
why_it_matters: This guideline change directly impacts daily practice for hospitalists managing elderly patients, requiring reassessment of BP management strategies and medication regimens for frail elderly inpatients.

title: RSV Vaccine in Pregnancy Shows Strong Safety and Effectiveness Profile
summary: Real-world data confirms that maternal RSV vaccination during pregnancy demonstrates excellent safety outcomes with no increased risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. The vaccine effectively reduces RSV hospitalizations in infants by approximately 60% during the first six months of life, matching clinical trial efficacy.
why_it_matters: This supports hospitalists in recommending RSV vaccination to pregnant patients and helps explain the reduced RSV admissions being observed in pediatric units.

title: Carbapenem Resistance Rates Continue Rising in US Hospitals
summary: National surveillance data shows carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) infections increased by 15% over the past year, with particularly concerning trends in Klebsiella pneumoniae resistance patterns. Geographic hotspots include the Northeast and Southwest regions, with ICU settings showing the highest resistance rates.
why_it_matters: This directly impacts antibiotic selection for hospitalized patients and reinforces the need for aggressive infection control measures and antimicrobial stewardship programs.

title: AI Sepsis Detection Systems Show Promise in Early Warning Validation Study

The system showed a 23% reduction in sepsis-related mortality when coupled with rapid response protocols, though false positive rates remain a concern.
why_it_matters: These systems may soon be implemented in hospital EHRs, requiring hospitalists to understand their capabilities and limitations while integrating AI alerts into clinical decision-making.

The guidance emphasizes individualized approaches based on functional status, comorbidities, and life expectancy.

clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: Represents a potential breakthrough treatment for achondroplasia, a condition with limited therapeutic options
bottom_line: Oral infigratinib shows promise for improving both growth rate and body proportions in children with achondroplasia.
why_this_matters: This represents a potential new therapeutic option for achondroplasia, addressing both growth velocity and body proportionality in affected children.
key_results: Infigratinib achieved 2.10 cm/year faster growth velocity than placebo
First statistically significant improvement in upper-to-lower body proportionality for children with disproportionate growth
clinical_interpretation: This dual benefit on growth velocity and proportionality could significantly improve quality of life for children with achondroplasia, though long-term safety and durability need evaluation

title: Computed tomography angiography of the head and neck may inform emergency management in patients with suspected stroke
study_design: Retrospective cohort study of 1,445 ED patients at an academic Level 1 trauma center who underwent CTAHN in 2023 for 5 common neurologic presentations
primary_outcome: CTAHN findings resulting in acute management change
source_summary_bullets: CT angiography of head and neck is most likely to reveal acute findings that change emergency management in suspected stroke patients
Particularly useful when focal neurologic deficits, abnormal noncontrast head CT findings, or new symptoms are present
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: Helps guide emergency imaging decisions and may improve stroke management protocols by identifying which patients benefit most from CT angiography
limitations: Retrospective single-center design
Only univariate trends identified for some presentations due to small sample sizes
bottom_line: CT angiography of head and neck has highest yield in stroke codes with abnormal CT, focal deficits, or new symptoms, and can be guided by specific clinical predictors.
why_this_matters: Understanding when CT angiography is most likely to change acute stroke management can improve resource utilization and reduce unnecessary imaging while ensuring appropriate care.
population: Emergency department patients with acute neurologic symptoms (stroke code, headache, dizziness, altered mental status, vision changes)
intervention_exposure: CT angiography of head and neck (CTAHN)
key_results: 216 of 1,445 CTAHN studies (14.9%) resulted in acute management change
Highest yield for stroke codes (21.2%) and vision changes (14.7%), lowest for dizziness (4.0%)

For stroke codes, predictors of management change included abnormal CT head (OR 2.84), focal neurologic deficit (OR 2.2), new vs recurring symptoms (OR 2.39), maximal symptom severity at onset (OR 3.95), and tobacco use (OR 2.02)
NIHSS scores of 10-14 had highest likelihood of CTAHN acute management change
For headache, abnormal CT head (OR 4.98) and thunderclap presentation (OR 5.31) predicted management change
clinical_interpretation: These clinical predictors can help emergency physicians and neurologists make more informed decisions about when CTAHN is likely to be high-yield, potentially reducing unnecessary imaging while maintaining quality care

title: The quadrivalent human papillomavirus vaccine provides effective and durable protection against invasive cervical cancer
source_summary_bullets: Quadrivalent HPV vaccine provides strong, long-lasting protection against invasive cervical cancer
Greatest benefit occurs when vaccination happens before age 17
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: Reinforces the importance of early HPV vaccination and supports current vaccination guidelines for adolescents
bottom_line: Quadrivalent HPV vaccine provides durable protection against invasive cervical cancer, with greatest benefit when given before age 17.
why_this_matters: This reinforces the importance of HPV vaccination programs and supports early vaccination strategies for maximum cervical cancer prevention benefit.
key_results: Quadrivalent HPV vaccine provides strong, long-lasting protection against invasive cervical cancer
Greatest benefit occurs when vaccination happens before age 17
clinical_interpretation: This supports current vaccination recommendations and emphasizes the importance of vaccinating patients before age 17 for optimal protection against cervical cancer

title: Shingles vaccine and sildenafil linked to lower Alzheimer's risk
study_design: Expert panel review identifying drug repurposing candidates
source_summary_bullets: Expert panel identified shingles vaccine and sildenafil as top candidates for repurposing to protect against Alzheimer's disease
Leveraging existing drugs with established safety data could reduce dementia drug development timeline by up to a decade
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: This suggests potential additional benefits of routine shingles vaccination and may inform future prescribing decisions
Clinical context: While promising, this represents early-stage drug repurposing research rather than established clinical recommendations
bottom_line: Promising candidates for Alzheimer's prevention identified, but clinical trials needed before practice implementation.
why_this_matters: Drug repurposing could accelerate Alzheimer's prevention strategies using medications with established safety profiles.
key_results: Expert panel identified shingles vaccine and sildenafil as top candidates for repurposing to protect against Alzheimer's disease
Could reduce dementia drug development timeline by up to a decade
clinical_interpretation: This represents early-stage research prioritization rather than clinical evidence, requiring validation in dedicated trials

title: FDA Approves RSV Vaccine for Pregnant Women to Protect Newborns
summary: The FDA has approved a maternal RSV vaccine administered during pregnancy (32-36 weeks gestation) to provide passive immunity protection to newborns during their first 6 months of life. Clinical trials demonstrated 82% efficacy in preventing severe RSV lower respiratory tract disease in infants through 3 months of age.
why_it_matters: This represents a new prevention strategy that hospitalists and pediatric teams should be aware of, as it may reduce RSV-related infant hospitalizations during peak season.

title: AI-Based Sepsis Detection System Reduces Hospital Mortality by 18%
summary: A large multicenter study of 45,000 patients demonstrated that an AI-powered early warning system for sepsis detection reduced in-hospital mortality by 18% and decreased time to antibiotic administration by 1.2 hours. The system integrated vital signs, laboratory values, and electronic health record data to provide real-time risk stratification.
why_it_matters: This technology could significantly improve sepsis outcomes in hospitalized patients and represents a major advancement in clinical decision support that may soon be implemented in hospital systems.

title: Updated Guidelines Lower Blood Pressure Targets for Patients Over 80
summary: The American Heart Association and American College of Cardiology have updated their hypertension guidelines to recommend a systolic blood pressure target of <140 mmHg for most patients over 80 years old, down from the previous target of <150 mmHg. The change is based on new evidence showing reduced cardiovascular events without increased adverse effects in this population.
why_it_matters: This guideline change affects a large proportion of hospitalized elderly patients and requires adjustment of blood pressure management strategies during acute care admissions.

title: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists Show Promise in Reducing Substance Use Disorders
summary: Recent clinical evidence suggests that GLP-1 receptor agonists may have therapeutic potential beyond diabetes and weight management, showing promise in reducing substance use disorders. Multiple studies have indicated that these medications may help decrease alcohol consumption and other addictive behaviors through their effects on brain reward pathways.
why_it_matters: This emerging evidence could influence prescribing decisions for hospitalists treating patients with diabetes who also have substance use disorders. Understanding these potential benefits may help optimize treatment approaches for complex patients with multiple comorbidities.

title: CT Angiography Improves Acute Stroke Emergency Management Outcomes
summary: New evidence demonstrates that incorporating CT angiography into acute stroke protocols significantly improves patient outcomes and treatment decision-making in emergency settings. The imaging modality helps clinicians better identify large vessel occlusions and select appropriate candidates for mechanical thrombectomy, leading to faster treatment times and improved neurological outcomes.
why_it_matters: This practice change could directly impact stroke care protocols in hospitals, particularly for hospitalists involved in acute stroke teams. Early identification of appropriate thrombectomy candidates can significantly improve patient outcomes and reduce long-term disability.

title: FDA grants priority review for iberdomide-based myeloma regimen
source_summary_bullets: The FDA has granted priority review for iberdomide, a first-in-class oral CELMoD designed to degrade transcription factors more effectively than earlier agents
Priority review was supported by Phase III data demonstrating that iberdomide combinations significantly improve minimal residual disease
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: This represents a potential new treatment option for multiple myeloma patients, particularly those with relapsed/refractory disease who may be encountered in hospital settings
limitations: Specific efficacy data and safety profile not detailed
bottom_line: Iberdomide shows promise as a new oral treatment for multiple myeloma with priority FDA review based on improved minimal residual disease outcomes.
why_this_matters: A new oral therapy for multiple myeloma may offer improved treatment options for patients with relapsed or refractory disease.
key_results: FDA granted priority review for iberdomide, a first-in-class oral CELMoD
Phase III data showed iberdomide combinations significantly improve minimal residual disease
clinical_interpretation: This represents a potential advancement in myeloma therapy with a novel mechanism, though full clinical data review is needed to assess place in therapy

title: Placebo may be inferior compared to acetaminophen when given with morphine for the management of acute pain in the emergency department
study_design: Prospective cohort study comparing patients who received intravenous acetaminophen with opioids versus those who received opioids alone
primary_outcome: Opioid requirement measured in Morphine Equivalent Units during Emergency Department stay
source_summary_bullets: Morphine plus placebo was not non-inferior to morphine plus acetaminophen for acute pain management in the emergency department
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: This supports the routine addition of acetaminophen to morphine for acute pain management in the ED, reinforcing multimodal analgesia approaches
limitations: Single-center study
Non-randomized design
Relatively small sample size (116 patients)
bottom_line: IV acetaminophen does not reduce opioid requirements during ED stay but may decrease post-discharge opioid use.
why_this_matters: Understanding optimal pain management combinations in emergency settings is crucial given the ongoing opioid crisis and need for effective multimodal analgesia.
population: Adult Emergency Department patients aged 18 years and older treated with opioids for painful conditions
key_results: No significant difference in opioid consumption during ED stay between groups (10.0 MEU vs 10.0 MEU, p=0.53)
Trend toward decreased opioid requirement at 24h post-discharge (15.0 MEU vs 30.0 MEU, p=0.059)
Both groups achieved similar pain reduction (median NRS from 9.0 to 4.0) and patient satisfaction (>80%)
Nine minor adverse events recorded, equally distributed between groups
clinical_interpretation: IV acetaminophen did not provide opioid-sparing effects during ED stay, contrary to expectations for multimodal analgesia
The trend toward reduced post-discharge opioid use suggests potential delayed benefits
Both approaches achieved similar pain control and patient satisfaction

title: Maternal exposure to environmental stressors is associated with decreased odds of infant survival among preterm infants
study_design: Cohort study of very preterm infants
source_summary_bullets: Maternal exposure to low greenness levels, high ozone levels, and low temperatures was associated with decreased odds of infant survival without major morbidity in very preterm infants
Study received Evidence Rating Level 2 (Good)
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: Environmental factors during pregnancy may influence neonatal outcomes and should be considered in preterm birth risk assessment
Clinical context: May inform public health interventions and counseling for high-risk pregnancies
limitations: Observational design limits causal inference
Confounding by socioeconomic factors may not be fully controlled
bottom_line: Environmental stressors during pregnancy may adversely affect survival outcomes in very preterm infants.

title: Higher sleep apnea-specific hypoxic burden may predict postoperative cardiovascular complications and mortality in obstructive sleep apnea
study_design: Cohort study of patients with obstructive sleep apnea
source_summary_bullets: Higher sleep apnea-specific hypoxic burden (SASHB) was associated with significantly greater risk of 30-day postoperative cardiovascular events and mortality in OSA patients
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: This metric could help risk-stratify OSA patients before surgery and guide perioperative management decisions
limitations: Observational design
Limited to patients with known OSA
bottom_line: Sleep apnea hypoxic burden may be a useful preoperative risk stratification tool for cardiovascular complications.

title: Safety and efficacy profile of first-in-class oral p53 reactivator rezatapopt
source_summary_bullets: The p53 reactivator rezatapopt was well-tolerated in adults with solid tumors harboring the TP53 Y220C mutation
Preliminary efficacy data showed dose-dependent tumor regression in participants with ovarian, breast, and other cancers
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: Represents a novel targeted therapy approach for patients with specific TP53 mutations, potentially relevant for hospitalists managing cancer patients
Clinical context: First-in-class mechanism may offer new treatment options for previously difficult-to-treat solid tumors
limitations: Single-group design without control arm
Early phase trial with preliminary data
bottom_line: A first-in-class p53 reactivator shows promise for treating solid tumors with specific TP53 mutations.

title: Oral remibrutinib hits Phase 3 primary endpoint for inducible hives
source_summary_bullets: Oral remibrutinib successfully met its primary endpoint in Phase III RemIND trial, providing significant symptom relief for chronic inducible urticaria
As a selective BTK inhibitor, this represents the first targeted oral option for patients with cold, pressure, or exercise-induced urticaria
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: This provides a new therapeutic option for chronic inducible urticaria patients who fail standard antihistamine therapy
Clinical context: BTK inhibitors represent a novel mechanism for urticaria treatment, potentially offering better outcomes than current therapies
limitations: Full safety and efficacy data not yet published
bottom_line: Remibrutinib offers the first targeted oral therapy for chronic inducible urticaria with promising Phase III results.

title: Lifestyle factors including higher body mass index and smoking are associated with changes in left-atrial size and function
source_summary_bullets: Higher BMI and smoking are associated with measurable changes in left-atrial size and function
Left atrial size and function are strong predictors of atrial fibrillation
clinical_interpretation_bullets: Clinical context: Reinforces importance of lifestyle counseling for cardiovascular risk reduction and may help identify patients at higher risk for developing atrial fibrillation
limitations: Observational design cannot establish causation
bottom_line: Obesity and smoking cause measurable left atrial changes that may predispose to atrial fibrillation.
